title: Word Completion ({key:Ctrl-N} / {key:Ctrl-P})
subtitle: Type a few letters; let Vim finish the word.
summary: {key:Ctrl-N} and {key:Ctrl-P} trigger keyword completion from the current and other buffers. Once a menu is up, {key:Ctrl-N}/{key:Ctrl-P} walk the suggestions; {key:Ctrl-E} cancels; {key:Ctrl-Y} accepts.

blocks.text: Type a partial word, then {key:Ctrl-N} (next) or {key:Ctrl-P} (previous) to autocomplete from words already in scope. Vim has had this since the mid-90s — long before IDE autocomplete was standard.

blocks.title: Where Suggestions Come From
blocks.text:
{key:Ctrl-N}/{key:Ctrl-P} use the `complete` option (default `.,w,b,u,t`) to decide which sources to scan:

  • `.` — current buffer
  • `w` — buffers in other windows
  • `b` — other loaded buffers
  • `u` — unloaded buffers
  • `t` — tags
  • `i` — included files (via `include` regex)
  • `k` — dictionary file

Matches are scanned in the order listed, deduplicated, and shown in a popup menu (`completeopt` controls how). The menu navigation keys ({key:Ctrl-N}/{key:Ctrl-P}/{key:Ctrl-Y}/{key:Ctrl-E}) only apply *while the menu is visible*.
The default *just works* for code: pulls from the file you're editing plus open files. For something more powerful, see {key:Ctrl-X} sub-completions next.
